questions.question: Which is true about Java records in Java 17?
choices: Records are mutable by default; mark them 'final' to make them immutable.
Records are immutable, final, and provide implicit equals/hashCode/toString and accessor methods named like the component (e.g. id(), not getId()).
Records require manual implementations of equals and hashCode.
Records can be extended via 'extends' to add behaviour.
questions.explanation: Java records are immutable, implicitly final, and generate equals/hashCode/toString plus bare-name accessors (id(), not getId()). The naming difference matters for framework integration.

questions.question: Why is the switch over a sealed interface exhaustive without a default case?
choices: The Java compiler always allows missing default branches in switch expressions.
Because the sealed interface enumerates its permitted subtypes; the compiler can verify every case is covered and rejects new subtypes silently breaking the switch.
Because records are final, they can't add new alternatives.
Because pattern matching switch ignores missing cases at runtime.
questions.explanation: A sealed type declares its permitted subtypes. The compiler uses that closed set to verify exhaustiveness - and a new permitted subtype later forces every switch to add a case at compile time, removing the runtime surprise.

questions.question: You want to model a 'Customer' with id, name, and email, where the email must not be blank. Which approach is idiomatic in Java 17?
choices: A class with private fields and manual setters that throw on invalid input.
A record with a compact constructor that validates and canonicalises the email before assignment.
A class with public fields and external validation; records can't validate.
A sealed interface - records can't enforce invariants.
questions.explanation: The idiomatic Java 17 form is a record with a compact constructor that validates and canonicalises components. The compact form has no parameter list and field assignment happens implicitly at the end of the block.
